planting.note: Direct sow in fall for spring germination or divide established rhizomatous colonies in spring; round-leaf ragwort grows naturally in moist to well-drained, slightly alkaline to neutral, humus-rich soil in partial to deep shade in deciduous woodland settings.

directions: Plant in moist to well-drained, slightly alkaline to neutral, humus-rich soil in partial to deep shade; round-leaf ragwort is one of the most useful shade-tolerant native groundcovers of the eastern woodland, with attractive, rounded, evergreen basal leaves that persist year-round and bright yellow daisy-like flowers in early spring — providing crucial early-season nectar when few other flowers are open. The plant spreads by rhizomes to form a dense, weed-suppressing mat in moist, shaded conditions. A superb native lawn substitute or ground layer plant under deciduous trees where it combines evergreen winter foliage interest with important early spring pollinator value. The early flowers are a critical nectar source for queen bumblebees and early mining bees emerging in late winter. One of the best native shade groundcovers for the entire eastern region in zones 4–9.

planting.note: Direct sow in spring in dry to moderately moist, sandy, slightly acidic soil in partial shade to full sun; elephant's foot grows naturally in dry to moist, sandy, open pine flatwoods and woodland edges of the Gulf and Atlantic Coastal Plain.

directions: Plant in dry to moderately moist, sandy, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade to full sun; elephant's foot is a distinctive native of the southeastern Coastal Plain, named for its unusually large, rough, basal leaves that spread flat along the ground like the wrinkled feet of an elephant. The plant sends up tall, branched stems in summer topped with small, composite lavender-purple flower heads enclosed in leaf-like bracts — an unusual and ornamentally distinctive inflorescence structure. The flowers attract numerous small bees and butterflies. A shade-tolerant native that provides interest in dry, sandy, partial-shade conditions of pine flatwoods and open woodland gardens in zone 9a. A native plant that is extremely common as a 'weed' but underappreciated as an ornamental.

planting.note: Direct sow or plant divisions in spring in well-drained, sandy, saline to neutral, dry soil in full sun; sea ox-eye daisy is highly salt-tolerant and grows naturally in coastal salt marshes, dune edges, and brackish waterway margins from Virginia to Texas.

directions: Plant in well-drained, sandy, saline-tolerant to neutral soil in full sun in coastal gardens; sea ox-eye daisy is a native, low-growing, aromatic subshrub of coastal salt marshes and tidal flats along the Atlantic and Gulf Coastal Plain — one of the most salt-spray and salt-soil tolerant native wildflowers of the coastal zone. The bright yellow daisy flowers with their distinctive button-like centers bloom prolifically from summer through fall, providing extended season color and nectar for coastal butterflies and native bees. The grayish, succulent-like leaves reflect the plant's adaptation to the harsh saline conditions of coastal habitats. In the garden, it spreads by rhizomes to form a dense, salt-resistant ground cover that excels in coastal landscapes where few other flowering perennials survive. An outstanding plant for Zone 9a coastal Gulf Coast gardens.

planting.note: Direct sow in fall in dry, sandy, strongly acidic, infertile soil in full sun; grass-leaved golden aster grows naturally in dry, sandy pine flatwoods and sandhills of the Coastal Plain from Virginia to Texas, where it is one of the most reliable and abundant wildflowers of the longleaf pine ecosystem.

directions: Plant in dry, sandy, strongly acidic, infertile, well-drained soil in full sun; grass-leaved golden aster is one of the most characteristic and abundant native wildflowers of the longleaf pine flatwoods ecosystem, producing silvery, silky, grass-like leaves from a rosette and bright golden-yellow daisy flowers in late summer and fall. The silvery-gray leaf surfaces (from dense silky hairs) give the plant a distinctive shimmer in the sun that led to the common name 'silkgrass.' It is adapted to the challenging conditions of deep, white, nutrient-poor sand in full, hot summer sun — conditions that discourage most garden plants. The bright fall flowers are an important late-season nectar source for skippers, hairstreak butterflies, and native bees in the coastal plain. One of the most drought-tolerant and heat-tolerant native wildflowers for zone 9a sandy gardens.

planting.note: Direct sow in spring in dry, sandy, slightly acidic to neutral, disturbed soil in full sun; camphorweed is a native annual or biennial that self-sows prolifically and is commonly found colonizing dry, disturbed, sandy roadsides and open habitats along the Gulf Coast.

directions: Allow to self-sow in dry, sandy, slightly acidic to neutral, open, disturbed soil in full sun; camphorweed is one of the most abundant and ecologically valuable native annual wildflowers of the Gulf Coastal Plain, immediately colonizing bare, disturbed, sandy soil with a profusion of bright yellow daisy-like flowers from late summer through fall. The aromatic, camphor-scented foliage repels deer and rabbits. The plant provides extraordinarily rich late-season nectar for monarch butterflies during their fall migration — a role of critical conservation value along the Gulf Coast migration corridor. Despite its weedy tendency in disturbed areas, camphorweed is a prized monarch nectar plant and should be intentionally maintained in natural garden areas and meadow plantings where its valuable pollinator role can be appreciated. Self-sows reliably to perpetuate populations without replanting.
